reason:
The statement says the constraints can be 'f_j(x) <= beta_j (or = beta_j)' but the iterative construction only writes the sublevel-set version: K_1 = {x in K_0 : f_1(x) <= beta_1}, ..., K_m = C. For equality constraints f_j(x) = beta_j, the construction should use K_j = {x in K_{j-1} : f_j(x) = beta_j}, and the proof should note that Node 1.2.1 covers both sublevel sets AND level sets, so the single-constraint result applies regardless of whether the j-th constraint is an inequality or equality.

Additionally, the proof should note that if some constraints are equalities, one could alternatively decompose each equality f_j = beta_j into two inequalities f_j <= beta_j and -f_j <= -beta_j, which would use two steps (costing dimension 2). The fact that the single-constraint result (1.2.1) directly handles level sets means equalities cost only 1 each, which is tighter. This distinction matters and should be made explicit.

FIX: In the iterative construction, explicitly allow K_i to be defined via either sublevel or level sets depending on the type of the i-th constraint, and note that 1.2.1 applies to both cases.
